<!-- Generated by scripts/sync_chebfun_examples.py. -->
<!-- Source: https://www.chebfun.org/examples/opt/GlobalMinimum.html -->

<div class="chebfun-import chebfun-example-import">
<div class='page-header'>
<h1>The global minimum of a complicated function</h1>
<h2>Alex Townsend, March 2013 in <a href='../'>opt</a><span><a href='/examples/opt/GlobalMinimum.m'>download</a><span class='sep-sm'>&middot;</span><a href='//github.com/chebfun/examples/blob/master/opt/GlobalMinimum.m'>view on GitHub</a></span></h2>
</div>

<div id='content' class="col-sm-12 example" role="main">
<h3 id="the-siam-100-dollar-100-digit-challenge">The SIAM 100-Dollar, 100-Digit Challenge</h3>
<p>In February 2002, an article in SIAM News by Nick Trefethen set a challenge to solve ten problems each to ten digits of precision (the solution of each problem was a real number) [1].  One of the problems was to find the global minimum of this complicated function.</p>
<pre class="mcode-input">f = @(x,y) exp(sin(50*x)) + sin(60*exp(y)) + sin(70*sin(x)) +...
    sin(sin(80*y)) - sin(10*(x+y)) + (x.^2+y.^2)./4;
x = linspace(-1,1);
[xx, yy] = meshgrid(x);
surf(xx, yy, f(xx,yy)),
title('The complicated function')</pre>

<p><img src="../../../images/opt/GlobalMinimum_01.png" class="figure chebfun-figure" alt=""></p>
<p>Since the term $(x^2+y^2)/4$ grows away from $(0,0)$ while the other terms remain bounded, it can be shown that the global minimum occurs in $[-1,1]^2$ [2].</p>
<p>The function is complicated and oscillatory, but of rank $4$, as can be seen by rearranging its terms and using the identity $\sin(a+b) = \sin(a)\cos(b) + \cos(a)\sin(b)$.</p>
<pre class="mcode-input">g = chebfun2(f);
fprintf('Rank of function = %u\n', rank(g))</pre>

<pre class="mcode-output">Rank of function = 4
</pre>

<p>For details about what we mean by the rank of a function see [3]. The minimum was found in [2] to 10,000 digits, and here are the first 16:</p>
<pre class="mcode-input">exact = -3.306868647475237;</pre>

<p>We can compute this minimum using Chebfun2. The minimum is correct to 13 digits.</p>
<pre class="mcode-input">Y = min2(g); fprintf('Computed global minimum = %1.16f\n', Y)
fprintf('Error in Chebfun2 minimum = %1.4e\n', abs(Y(1) -exact))</pre>

<pre class="mcode-output">Computed global minimum = -3.3068686474747913
Error in Chebfun2 minimum = 4.4587e-13
</pre>

<p>Here is the full four-line code and how long it takes:</p>
<pre class="mcode-input">s = tic;
f = @(x,y) exp(sin(50*x)) + sin(60*exp(y)) + sin(70*sin(x)) + sin(sin(80*y)) -...
    sin(10*(x+y)) + (x.^2+y.^2)./4;
g = chebfun2(f);
[Y, X] = min2(g);
t = toc(s);

fprintf('Total time taken = %1.4fs\n',t)</pre>

<pre class="mcode-output">Total time taken = 0.2693s
</pre>

<p>Here is the plot of the minimum in a contour plot:</p>
<pre class="mcode-input">contour(g), hold on, plot(X(1), X(2), '.k', 'markersize', 14), hold off</pre>

<p><img src="../../../images/opt/GlobalMinimum_02.png" class="figure chebfun-figure" alt=""></p>
<p>To see that the computed point is the global minimum we make the following plot:</p>
<pre class="mcode-input">plot(g), hold on, plot3(X(1),X(2),Y,'.k','markersize',20)
zlim([-10 10]), view(-24.5,4)</pre>

<p><img src="../../../images/opt/GlobalMinimum_03.png" class="figure chebfun-figure" alt=""></p>
<h3 id="references">References</h3>
<ol>
<li>
<p>Lloyd N. Trefethen, A 100-Dollar, 100-Digit Challenge, <em>SIAM News</em>, 35    (2002).</p>
</li>
<li>
<p>Folkmar Bornemann, Dirk Laurie, Stan Wagon and J&ouml;rg Waldvogel, <em>The SIAM    100-Digit Challenge: A Study in High-Accuracy Numerical Computing</em>, SIAM,    2004.</p>
</li>
<li>
<p>A. Townsend and L. N. Trefethen, An extension of Chebfun to two    dimensions, <em>SIAM Journal on Scientific Computing</em>, 35 (2013), C495-C518.</p>
</li>
</ol></div>
        </div>
    </div>
</div>
</div>
